messages.content: Recently, there has been a growing interest in using B-spline collocation methods for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), which are able to achieve a high level of accuracy, [27] solved the Bona–Smith family of Boussinesq equation type by Quintic B-spline collocation method, [28] also studied the coupled-BBM system with quintic B-spline collocation method, [29] used collocation method based on cubic B-splines for solving the generalized regularized long wave equation, [30] applied  finite difference formula and the cubic B-splines for solving the inverse system of Burgers equations, [31] have solved the nonlinear Korteweg-de Vries–Burgers' equation by method of Galerkin using quartic B-splines, [32] have solved the Kuramoto–Sivashinsky equation by collocation method using quintic B-spline, [33] have solved the generalized equal width (GEW) wave equation by using a quintic B-spline collocation algorithm, [34] have solved the modified equal width wave (MEW) equation based on Subdomain finite element method with quartic B-splines.

These recent studies demonstrate the effectiveness of B-spline collocation methods for solving various types of PDEs. The use of B-spline collocation allows for high accuracy in the solutions, making it a promising approach for numerical simulations. The choice of the degree of the B-splines, such as quintic or quartic, depends on the specific problem being solved and the desired accuracy.

In [27], the Bona-Smith family of Boussinesq equation type was solved using the quintic B-spline collocation method. Similarly, [28] used the same method to study the coupled-BBM system.

For the generalized regularized long wave equation, [29] employed a collocation method based on cubic B-splines. [30] used finite difference formula and cubic B-splines to solve the inverse system of Burgers equations.

In [31], the nonlinear Korteweg-de Vries–Burgers' equation was solved using the method of Galerkin with quartic B-splines. Similarly, [32] solved the Kuramoto–Sivashinsky equation using collocation method with quintic B-splines. [33] tackled the generalized equal width (GEW) wave equation using a quintic B-spline collocation algorithm.

Finally, [34] solved the modified equal width wave (MEW) equation using the Subdomain finite element method with quartic B-splines.

Overall, these studies highlight the versatility and accuracy of B-spline collocation methods for solving a wide range of PDEs, demonstrating their potential in various fields of scientific research and engineering applications.

Unfortunately, for the WBK shallow water equations, like the different partial differential models, it is difficult to provide an exact solution, and it may be impossible most of the time. Therefore, researchers resort to providing approximate solutions using analytical or numerical methods. Lately, numerous methods have been presented by many researchers for the solution of  the coupled of nonlinear WBK problems such as, [6] used the bifurcation method and qualitative theory of dynamical systems, [7] applied  homotopy analysis method, [8] used homotopy Perturbation scheme, [9] applied finite difference method by Radial Basis Function-Pseudospectral method, [10] by variational iteration method (VIM), [11] applied Exp-function method, [12] proposed differential transformation method, [13] used Adomian decomposition method, [14] utilized Yang transformation coupled with the Adomian technique. [15] used hybrid approach to construct the approximate solution of the coupled nonlinear WBK, and [16] proposed a time-space Chebyshev pseudo-spectral method based on delta-shaped basis function is developed to solve generalized equal width equation [17]. [18] Proposed equal width equation using polynomial scaling functions.

Due to the difficulty in obtaining exact solutions for the WBK shallow water equations, researchers have turned to approximate solutions using various analytical and numerical methods. Some of the recent methods used for solving the coupled nonlinear WBK problems are as follows:

In [6], the bifurcation method and qualitative theory of dynamical systems were applied to provide approximate solutions. [7] utilized the homotopy analysis method, while [8] used the homotopy perturbation scheme. [9] employed the finite difference method with the Radial Basis Function-Pseudospectral method.

The variational iteration method (VIM) was applied in [10], while the Exp-function method was used in [11] to solve the coupled nonlinear WBK problems. [12] proposed the differential transformation method, and [13] used the Adomian decomposition method. [14] utilized the Yang transformation coupled with the Adomian technique.

A hybrid approach was used in [15] to construct the approximate solution of the coupled nonlinear WBK. In [16], a time-space Chebyshev pseudo-spectral method based on delta-shaped basis functions was developed to solve the generalized equal width equation. [17] proposed a polynomial scaling function for the equal width equation in [18].

These studies demonstrate the variety of methods that have been employed to approximate solutions for the coupled nonlinear WBK problems, showing the active research interest in this area. These methods offer different advantages and may be suitable for different types of problems and desired accuracy levels.

Once the system of equations for the WBK shallow water equations has been simplified and the functions values of δ and c have been eliminated, the resulting system can be solved using any algorithm suitable for solving systems of equations.

The numerical solution can be obtained by evaluating the vectors δ_j^n and c_j^n over time using recurrence relations. These recurrence relations describe the update of the variables at each time step based on their previous values and the system equations. By iteratively updating the variables, the numerical solution of the system can be determined.

The specific form of the recurrence relations will depend on the numerical method being used to solve the system. Different algorithms may have different approaches for updating the variables at each time step. It is important to choose a reliable and accurate algorithm that suits the problem at hand.

By evaluating the vectors δ_j^n and c_j^n over time using the recurrence relations, the numerical solution of the WBK shallow water equations can be obtained, providing an approximation to the behavior of the system.

To obtain the numerical solution of the system, you can evaluate the vectors δ_j^n and c_j^n over time using recurrence relations. These recurrence relations define how the values of δ_j^n and c_j^n at each time step are updated based on their previous values and the equations of the system.

By iteratively applying the recurrence relations, you can calculate the values of δ_j^n and c_j^n at each time step, providing the numerical solution of the system. The specific form of the recurrence relations will depend on the structure of the equations and the chosen numerical method.

It is important to choose a reliable numerical algorithm and ensure that the time step size and convergence criteria are appropriately selected to obtain accurate and stable results.
Haar method

The Haar method, also known as the Haar wavelet method, is a numerical method used for solving differential equations. It is based on the Haar wavelet basis functions, which are piecewise constant functions with compact support.

In the Haar method, the differential equation is first discretized by dividing the domain into a grid of points. The Haar wavelet basis functions are then used to approximate the solution at these grid points. The key idea is to represent the solution as a linear combination of the Haar wavelet basis functions, with unknown coefficients.

The coefficients in the Haar wavelet expansion are determined by enforcing the differential equation as well as any boundary or initial conditions. This can be done by transforming the differential equation into a system of algebraic equations, typically using a collocation or Galerkin method.

Once the coefficient values are obtained, the approximate solution can be reconstructed by summing the Haar wavelet basis functions weighted by their corresponding coefficients.

The Haar method has been successfully applied to various types of differential equations, including ordinary differential equations, partial differential equations, and integral equations. It is known for its simplicity and computational efficiency, especially for problems with sharp discontinuities or oscillatory behavior.

However, like any numerical method, the accuracy and convergence of the Haar method depend on the grid size and the smoothness of the solution. It may require refined grids and adaptive strategies for problems with irregular or rapidly changing solutions.
Numerical Solution of the Whitham-Broer-Kaup Shallow Water Equation by Quartic B-Spline Collocation Method

To numerically solve the Whitham-Broer-Kaup (WBK) shallow water equation using the quartic B-spline collocation method, we can follow these steps:

1. Discretize the spatial domain: 
   Divide the spatial domain into a set of uniformly spaced grid points, denoted as {x_j}, where j = 1, 2, ..., N. The step size h can be determined based on the desired spatial resolution.

2. Approximate the solution using quartic B-spline basis functions:
   The quartic B-spline basis functions, denoted as B_i(x), can be constructed based on the grid points. These basis functions have compact support and are piecewise polynomials of degree 3 within each interval.

3. Express the solution and its derivatives using the quartic B-spline basis functions:
   Approximate the solution u(x, t) and its spatial derivatives (e.g., u_x, u_xx) as linear combinations of the quartic B-spline basis functions:
   
   u(x, t) ≈ Σ u_j(t) * B_j(x),
   u_x(x, t) ≈ Σ u'_j(t) * B_j(x),
   u_xx(x, t) ≈ Σ u''_j(t) * B_j(x),

   where u_j(t), u'_j(t), and u''_j(t) are the unknown coefficients at each grid point.

4. Discretize the time domain:
   Divide the time domain into a set of time levels, denoted as {t_n}, where n = 1, 2, ..., M. The time step size Δt can be determined based on the desired temporal resolution.

5. Apply the quartic B-spline collocation method:
   Substitute the approximations into the WBK equation and its derivatives. This yields a system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) in terms of the unknown coefficients u_j(t), u'_j(t), and u''_j(t).

   For example, the WBK equation can be discretized as:

   u_t + (1/6) (u*u_xxx + 6*u_x*u_xx + 2*u*u_x) = 0.

   By imposing the collocation condition at each grid point, i.e., evaluating the equation at x = x_j, we obtain a set of simultaneous ODEs that can be solved numerically.

6. Solve the system of ODEs:
   Integrate the system of ODEs over time using numerical methods such as the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method or implicit methods like the backward differentiation formula (BDF). This yields the time evolution of the coefficients u_j(t), u'_j(t), and u''_j(t).

7. Reconstruct the solution:
   Finally, reconstruct the approximate solution u(x, t) at any point in the spatial domain by summing the contributions from all grid points:

   u(x, t) = Σ u_j(t) * B_j(x).

   The accuracy of the numerical solution depends on the spatial and temporal resolutions (grid size and time step size) chosen, as well as the smoothness of the exact solution.

It is important to note that the above steps provide a general outline of the numerical procedure using the quartic B-spline collocation method. The specific implementation details may vary depending on the software or programming language being used.
finite element method for the Whitham-Broer-Kaup Shallow Water Equation

To solve the Whitham-Broer-Kaup (WBK) shallow water equation using the finite element method (FEM), you can follow these steps:

1. Discretize the spatial domain:
   Divide the spatial domain into a set of non-overlapping elements or finite elements. The number of elements and their sizes can be chosen based on the desired spatial resolution. Each element is defined by a set of nodes or grid points.

2. Choose an appropriate finite element space:
   Select a suitable basis function for each element. Common choices include linear, quadratic, or cubic shape functions. The basis functions should be able to represent the local behavior of the solution within each element.

3. Formulate the weak form of the WBK equation:
   Multiply the WBK equation by a suitable test function, integrate it over the domain, and integrate by parts if necessary. This yields a variational or weak form of the equation.

4. Apply the Galerkin approximation:
   Approximate the solution and the test function using the chosen finite element basis functions. Substitute these approximations into the weak form of the equation, resulting in a discrete system of equations.

5. Assemble the global system:
   Combine the individual element equations and boundary conditions into a global system of equations. This involves assembling the element-wise contributions into a global stiffness matrix and a global load vector.

6. Apply boundary conditions:
   Incorporate the essential (Dirichlet) and natural (Neumann or Robin) boundary conditions into the global system by modifying the appropriate rows and columns of the stiffness matrix and the load vector.

7. Solve the linear system:
   Solve the resulting linear system of equations, typically using numerical methods such as direct solvers (e.g., Gaussian elimination) or iterative solvers (e.g., conjugate gradient, GMRES).

8. Recover the nodal solution:
   Evaluate the solution at the nodes or grid points to obtain the nodal values of the approximate solution.

9. Post-process the solution:
   Analyze and visualize the computed solution. This may involve computing derived quantities of interest, such as gradients, fluxes, or integral quantities.

It is important to note that the accuracy of the FEM solution depends on factors such as the choice of finite element space, mesh quality, and the smoothness of the exact solution. Care should be taken to choose an appropriate mesh and finite element degree to ensure accurate results.

The above steps provide a general framework for solving the WBK shallow water equation using FEM. However, the specific implementation details may vary depending on the software or programming language being used.
